speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 6. Survivor is Evy Woods. The day is April 16, 1996. We left off on the last tape. You mentioned you were reunited with your mother and found out she had been in the hospital with lockjaw. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she gradually got better. And I, I continued to stay. I want to tell you, though, about a specific episode at the, at the orphanage that was really important to me. We often wonder at what point in our lives any kind of ethic or morality forms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm one of those people. I don't believe in God. I don't believe in anything. I believe only in the chair I'm sitting on with its four legs. I do believe in ethic and morality, which I know comes from religion. But that's still all there is. I believe in being 100% correct. I-- because of this lying that I had to do during those years, I see red when anything involving lying comes into my domain. It's just something I can't tolerate. I can't live with it. It's like losing yourself, finally, finally coming unraveled. Well, came a day in the winter, when [NON-ENGLISH] said, hurry, hurry, we have to go outside at the orphanage. School was over. We had no paper, no pencil. We did math in our heads, five digits times three digits. By now, the orphanage had been taken over by the communists. And the Jewish teachers were out. So now we had to talk Russian in the place instead of Yiddish. We used to read and write Yiddish. And still, life continued. They allowed the orphanage to exist. We ran over across the wide street. This was on a river, as a matter of fact. And there was a wooden bar running along the footpath on the opposite side of the broad street separating the river and it. And each day, apparently, they marched German prisoners to and from some work detail. And these kids had found out about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, quick, quick, pick up stones. You've got to pick up many stones. And I said, OK. But why? Why? Sometimes it was hard to understand the language. I couldn't do every word in Yiddish, only about every second word in Yiddish. And she said, we have to throw the stones. Well, we got up to that railing of-- that went all along as long the eye could, could see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we stood on the side of the railing where the street was and the little sickly looking batch of soldiers in their original uniforms still from that time-- now we're talking two years later or more-- some with coats, some without, some with torn uniforms. It was grim. The condition of shoes, such as they were, or used to be, are, are no longer. And it was bitter cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, throw, throw. Get started. And I said, why, why do we have to throw? She said, these are the German soldiers, the people who killed our families. This is why we're orphans. That's why we don't have a mother or a father. And that's why you don't have a father. This had no connection for me. I remember staring at this group and thinking, this has nothing to do with common sense. Why would I throw stones at these people? These aren't the very ones who did that. I can't do it. And I, I wouldn't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, now it came back to get me. That's because you're German. You don't want to throw those because you're German. Didn't matter to me what, what she was saying. The point was I remember. I knew nothing was going to leave my hand to make that happen. In other words, I was aware of feeling sorry. The first thing I noticed was the skimpiness of the uniforms vis-a-vis the weather and the snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would you hurt somebody like that? You see, I don't know if this is something we're born with, although I can't imagine that all the Nazis were born bad. I don't believe that. I think it's a learned thing, step by step. First you kick the dog or the cat, and from there you progress into a kind of-- I even think that a lot of children today are the children of no conscience, I think is what's wrong there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember this. And I held on to that. And it is the only thing that I've ever held onto to this day, that what is right is right. And I have to act in a rightful way. My mother kidnapped me from that orphanage together with a friend of hers because I didn't want to leave. Because these were my friends. I had a place where I belonged. I was in the Pioneers. We sang Russian songs, [NON-ENGLISH] and I don't know what. I mean, I belonged there. I had never belonged to anything. But she said, we're going home to meet our own people and to see if my father would surely be waiting in Berlin. Because repatriation trains were by now going back to Germany on and off. But the NKVD, later KGB, had my mother in their grip, because she spoke languages. They weren't going to let her go. And she knew that. And actually, if I remember, we were taking the place of some other people who were known to some of her Russian Jewish friends, educated in Europe, who said, we found a name or two of people that won't be there for that repatriation train. And you can substitute for that cattle train. They're always the same cattle trains. They just recycle them. And we got on, even though the man looked at us critically as he let us on the train. And we finally were on our way back to Germany, which took weeks and weeks, because typhus broke out. There was nothing to drink. The people drank machine oil from the locomotive and I don't know what. The dead people in the, in the cattle car, we had to throw out, you know, every day. There was no bathroom. There was no nothing. And I had a coat that someone had made for me out of a plaid blanket. And someone stole my coat while I fell asleep. I mean, you had to keep everything close to you. It's always the same, this camp, that camp, you know. It's always the same. The problems are the same. And then the petrifying thing, my mother goes off to try to find something to eat and-- you know, anything. And the train gets in motion. And I know I'm going to go to Timbuktu, and my mother is going to be left behind. I'd already had enough of this. And somehow, by a miracle, she appeared. That's all I know. And we did get after-- we were incubated in West-- in East Germany for a time because of this typhus. We had to contact our same old rescuers, Dr. Abegg and Hilda [NON-ENGLISH], later Arnold. And they, again, provided the money for a train ticket. And again, we had to leave that place illegally because the quarantine time wasn't up. However, we had sense enough to know if we stayed, we would soon get the typhus. And so we made a dash for it. The interesting thing is we're highly law-abiding people. This had no impact on our conduct later. In fact, it was hard enough to have to do that during those times.

speaker: interviewee
text: So back to Berlin. I had never been to school. I am 10 years old. We had to lie about when we came. We had to say it was 1947, because the, the deadline for having been returned to Berlin and being eligible to immigrate was set at 1947. God knows who set this. I don't know. It was really what year, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948. And that's why we get a little mixed up sometimes. How many years was it and where was it? But from the photographs and my mother's writing on the back, one sees it's always 1948. And the same people helped us. And my mother sends me off to Sweden to stay with this acting couple, because they were taking in poor souls like me, while my mother set up a place to live. And I mean, we had to start somewhere. We had no man in our family who protected us or took care of us. And she went to work for the Americans-- because she could speak English-- for the immigration people, IRO, and was paid partially in US dollars, which made it fairly substantial amount of money. And I stayed in Sweden for about-- I don't know-- eight, nine months. I hated it because of what was wrong. These people didn't speak German. They only spoke Swedish. And I did not need, now, another language to get involved with. And although they were nice people, they were distant people. Swedes are not known for warmth. But they were kind people. And I did not go to school there. I just couldn't deal with any of that. So I still didn't go to school. Came finally back to Berlin and opted for an America immigration, because some relatives of ours had survived in Shanghai, and they were all going to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't ever remember if it's '50 or '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we first came to New York, New York City. And my mother said, we're not going to live with all these deadbeats at the HIAS that were lying around, weren't learning the language, weren't--

speaker: interviewer
text: HIAS is what?

speaker: interviewee
text: HIAS was one of the helping organizations. And I don't remember what it stands for-- Hebrew something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother just went and got a job because she could speak some English. And we got a rented room in some fleabag place. And she found one of her distant cousins who had come about 1940 via Holland somehow. And he was somewhat helpful. But they were not in any great shape, he and his wife. She had been in some camp and, and had been released, I think bought her way out because she was a, a well-known movie actress prior to that, had still some belongings that she could buy her way free out of the Camp Gurs, G-U-R-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you eventually settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a few months later, we decided we couldn't live here. My mother started to get sick again. And we went to San Francisco, because some cousin of hers, the Shanghai people that had survived-- and these were all people I never saw. I never knew any relative but my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, I was supposed to be-- you know, it's like being religious, suddenly. You just can't pull it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you now, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: By now I was-- came here-- 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you continued to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I finally went. We tried it in New York, and that was a hopeless pit. And she's always still pushing religion. You know, we've got to get connected with quote, 'our people.' I didn't do well getting connected with our people, not in Berlin, not in New York, and not in San Francisco.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were always the German, you know. In the first place, in Germany, when we got back, some rabbi says to me, what would you do with this piece of white bread if I gave it to you and put butter and ham on it? And the answer was eat it. My answer was eat it. Did, did you go on to high school and college?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we sort of had a tryout school. I was supposed to be in the eighth grade. And finally, we just put me there. And so I used some insane method of memorizing everything, because I had no other learning mechanism. You know, learning to read German was easy, because if you can say the alphabet, it's like Spanish. You can literally read 70% of what there is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I taught myself. I got by with this. I had an A in spelling. They couldn't figure it out. I was-- got by with it until one day I encountered a word that I had to memorize with German letters, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I thought, boy, I hope I can remember that thing when it comes around.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [NON-ENGLISH]-- and I came close to not making it. But we made it. Because it was the word called 'wholesale.' So that was tough, because I used a memorization method on everything. I never learned how to apply anything I learned. To this day, I use that method.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get married in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mistakenly, as so many people in the States have. Because I believed what I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time I was married in 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have one daughter, who was born in 1967. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was divorced. You know, often, you can stay in a relationship by yourself, but not once you have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you're remarried.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are you remarried to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am remarried to John S. Woods, somebody I knew for a long time before I even had my daughter, whom I met at General Electric. And we've been married 24 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what career have you-- or jobs? Most recent.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always call them jobs. Because I, I find this description, career, a laughable thing in these times. These times are such that from one minute to the next, you had this career, and now you have that career. There is no such thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you pursue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had done work for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] corporate attorneys at GE, you know. And I had translated patents and done some research. And I did secretarial work. And I helped reactor salesmen learn enough German not to be boorish when they got to a foreign country. And what else?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your most recent job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most recently we had bought two frame stores. I mean picture framing. Some people think this means houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to Albuquerque?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the fate of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was arrested on the street, as I mentioned earlier, and was kept in-- what is that called-- isolation, solitary for some time, then on a work detail. Because he would not tell where my mother and I were. They always knew who belonged with you. That was the ID system that went back, you know, six different directions of your family. And it was enough if you were one-eighth Jewish. One Jewish grandparent was enough to sink your life. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot what I was saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was asking what was the fate of your father? What did you learn of his fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kept on that light duty from which he escaped three times. We found out through someone who was working taking in the Jews for a transport, a non-Jewish spouse of a Jewish person. And we knew then, finally, they gave up on him after the third escape. And his hair had turned gray in a week and all these stories that we heard. My mother tried to get laundry to him. My mother got two penciled notes from him, little scraps of paper that said, never worry about me. I'll, I'll make it. And then she saw him. She knew of a transport. And that same person told her that he was slated for that transport on August 4, 1943 to Auschwitz from, from the train station [NON-ENGLISH] which is in Zehlendorf, Berlin, actually in a very rural part, near a very-- part of an elegant part of town. And she ran there, as if she could, by some miracle, save him. But she was told by strangers, mind you, get away from here. People are running around with your ID picture because they presume that you may come forward. And she saw in the distance, a small grouping of people. I think this whole transport only had 99 people on it because it was so late. And saw a bunch of people in the background of another set of tracks, where all the cattle cars were. But she didn't know, you know, him from somebody else at that distance and somehow had to leave there. The records showed in 1990-- it took this long to get records, mind you-- that he left Berlin on the 4th of August and he arrived in Auschwitz on the 5th of August. There was none of this dawdling like the Eastern European trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always figure maybe a half an hour of life left, small man, over 40, glasses, and undernourished. Who has a need of this?

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the fate of your grandfather and your aunt, your mother's sister? What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know also-- we knew about the grandfather sooner. My mother's sister, during the time that Terezin or Theresienstadt, where she first was with her husband and my grandfather, that was a show camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until it became a transit camp and different kinds of people came there then, not just German-speaking. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She had given someone a postcard to mail, which even has a return address with a street in this camp. And it was addressed to my grandmother's neighbors, the same family I mentioned earlier several times, Baus. And it said, I wonder how you are. And I wonder-- haven't heard a long time from [NON-ENGLISH], which was a disguised way of saying my name, like little Evy, and her mother. And I hate to inform you that father was released from his suffering on May 4-- one day after my mother's birthday-- 1944, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yes, a year after he had been put there and that she had received a little package that was sent by them with some foodstuffs and thank you so much. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt, what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she, as it turned out-- again, I just found that out in recent years since the Black Books were compiled. And I again got verifications now. The Russians let go of some records. And the British got better organized with the records at [INAUDIBLE] and so forth. She had been-- oh, and then an eyewitness that I found in England, who was also a nurse, same hospital, same age group. That was my last stop. I had looked for decades for a nurse who might have survived. And she wrote to me in English and said, yes, I knew your aunt. I went to school with her at the hospital. And I last saw her in Bergen-Belsen. And at that point, she had been moved from Terezin, Theresienstadt to Auschwitz. Husband had been gassed. Father had died. So she knew-- had it all down correct. Didn't know she had married, but knew her married name now and had been on the foot march to Belsen, Bergen-Belsen. And this is where they met, with the barbed wire between them, because the other lady in error was put in a Dutch children's camp with her five-year-old son. And she said, the war is almost over, because she had just come there. She had been underground all this time in Berlin and was only lastly caught in December, '44 and said, you've got to hang on. And the-- my mother's sister said, no. I know I'm dying. And I just re-read this before you came to refresh my mind. And it's useless. Everybody's dead. My sister is dead. My husband's been gassed. Everybody's dead. No, I can't do this anymore. They were carrying a tree trunk on their shoulders, she and another woman, according to that letter, for whatever senseless reason. And the woman, whose name was [NON-ENGLISH], who is now named Murray, said she ordered her to come to that same fence tomorrow. Meet me tomorrow here, trying to give her incentive. And she said, no, I won't be. And she, she went back day after day after day and never saw her again. So we assumed that she was one of the, one of the people you saw later at Belsen, strewn on the grounds, I would guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you feel you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother is tough. [DRINKS] I feel nothing special about me, nor did a god save me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was luck. Because you see, a bomb could have hit us. My mother came close to that. Some of the people who helped us were killed by bombs. I think it was my mother's perseverance and drive and luck--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dream at all? And do you still dream about any of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a recurring dream in recent years, in fact. Drove me crazy. I never thought this would happen. I dreamed I was in a cattle car. And I dreamed the doors were flung open. This is hard. [CRYING] I just can't say that. [CRYING] Sometimes I have to write things when I can't say them. I dreamed-- [CRYING] I can't do it. [CRYING] I dreamed I was looking into this [INAUDIBLE] [CRYING] of what, what was supposed to be Auschwitz. [CRYING] There are people running madly alongside the thing. And it was nothing but flames everywhere. [CRYING] And I remember thinking in my dream, so this is what it looked like when my father got here. [CRYING] I don't know where I got that. [CRYING] I think I made it up. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I thought I had it made already through this whole thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bad question. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I used to think when all those dead bodies were flung around at Belsen, once I knew where my mother's sister had wound up, that one of those must be hers. My mother never watches this. She's afraid she'll recognize someone. But of course, we know she wouldn't recognize someone. People didn't even recognize each other there. But it's a fear. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] That's all I can say about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any advice that you would like to give for your children? You have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not going to be the advice you hoped for as a reply. This is not the advice that Jewish people want me to say. I don't make a good speaker to Jewish groups, because my advice is I'm grateful that my grandsons are not Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that's why I kept coloring my hair, because I, I felt safer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember my other rescuer, the psychologist Hilda [NON-ENGLISH] Arnold, used to say, how is this possible that you come away from all this without any problems? Because I felt really smug about that for decades.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it didn't last, just like houses of cards don't last, you know. Instead, there came panic attacks. And then there came-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything I hear in the news to me is frightening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I know this doesn't sound very hopeful. I hope these films aren't used for hopeful, for hopeful purposes. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, there's nothing to do with gods or anything else. It's just who survives and who doesn't, who murders and who doesn't. The gulf between us and some of those people is so wide, you know. It's all I can tell you. But I get alarmed sometimes when my daughter now talks about how she wishes we had raised her with religion. Her father was not Jewish. John Woods is not Jewish. And I think for me, that felt safe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she talks about wishing she had had something, because when she is in a problem situation that's severe, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it seems there's nothing to hold on to. And she's right. There isn't anything to hold on to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still have my mother. She has me. But I don't think any of us are here for long. It's just a blink of an eye. So I have nothing. I have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you're welcome. I wanted it to end on a smile instead of like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But thank you. And thank Mr. Spielberg for his idea. I think it was an unbelievable idea to give that much money to do this. Must have been a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
